text: Use return end functions make return explicit. exception anonymous functions short functions. Follow tidyverse style guide sole exception return rule .
code: # OK to not use returm \(x) is.na(sum(x, na.rm = TRUE))  as_numeric_comma <- function(x) {     as.numeric(gsub(",", ".", x)) }  # Use return str_simplify <- function(x) {     y <- stringr::str_to_lower(x)     y <- stringr::str_replace_all(y, " ", "_")     y <- stringr::str_squish(y)      return(y) }

title: Claude Code Configuration
text: Avoid using %>% |> favor |> Make sure pipe chains short (5) Never use single pipes (e.g. x |> mean()) Never pipe read function data cleaning Code logically organized easy understand: reading data, cleaning, joining, transforming, etc. Prefer using dplyr::rename dplyr::select character vectors improves standardization across tables Use modern tidyverse functions whenever possible.
code: # Correct dat <- readr::read_csv(...)  clean_dat <- dat |>   basic_clean() |>   parse_dates() |>   add_dimensions()  # Never dat <- readr::read_csv() |>   basic_clean() |>   parse_dates() |>   add_dimensions()  # Avoid mixing joins into data cleaning  # Bad left_join(x, y, by = "key") |>   basic_clean()  # Good new_data <- left_join(x, y, by = "key") clean_data <- basic_clean(new_data)  # The same applies to bind_rows  # Bad bind_rows(list_of_data) |>   basic_clean()  # When using bind_rows, use the .id argument instead of creating identifiers # for each data.frame  # Good series <- list(   "igmi" = igmi,   "ivar" = ivar,   "fipezap" = fipezap )  stacked <- bind_rows(series, .id = "source")  # Bad  bind_rows(   mutate(igmi, source = "igmi"),   mutate(ivar, source = "ivar"),   mutate(fipezap, source = "fipezap") )  # Try to order functions logically and efficiently  # Bad - filters after creating new columns dat |>   mutate(     y = log(x),     z = RcppRoll::roll_sumr(y, n = 12, align = "right")   ) |>   filter(group == "a") |>   rename(price = y)  # Good - renames first, filters, then calculates dat |>   rename(price = y) |>   filter(group == "a") |>   mutate(     z = RcppRoll::roll_sumr(log(price), n = 12, align = "right")   ) cols_select <- c("a", "b", "c") cols_rename <- c("new_a" = "a")   dat <- dat |>   dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(cols_select)) |>   dplyr::rename(dplyr::any_of(cols_rename))

title: Getting Started with realestatebr

Since realestatebr returns tibble default values, recommend using together dplyr package, though conversion data.table trivial. code defines common theme plots vignette required fully replicate code document. Despite , code entirely optional can omitted. realestatebr provides unified interface Brazilian real estate data multiple public sources. datasets returned tidy tibble objects.
code: library(realestatebr) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  color_palette <- c(   "#1E3A5F",   "#DD6B20",   "#2C7A7B",   "#D69E2E",   "#805AD5",   "#C53030" )  theme_series <- function() {   theme_minimal(     # swap for other font if needed     base_family = "Avenir",     base_size = 10     ) +     theme(       plot.title = element_text(size = 16),       panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),       panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),       axis.line.x = element_line(color = "gray10", linewidth = 0.5),       axis.ticks.x = element_line(color = "gray10", linewidth = 0.5),       axis.title.x = element_blank(),       legend.position = "bottom",       palette.color.discrete = color_palette     ) }

title: Getting Started with realestatebr
text: goal realestatebr provide unified interface Brazilian real estate data multiple public sources. datasets returned tidy tibble objects. package centered around key function: get_dataset(name, table) retrieves dataset name. Without table argument returns default table; use table select specific sub-table. Use get_dataset() main function retrieve datasets. order explore datasets available, use list_datasets() get_dataset_info(). list_datasets() returns catalogue available datasets tables. get_dataset_info() shows available tables metadata given dataset.
code: # Default table abecip <- get_dataset("abecip")  # Specific table sbpe <- get_dataset("abecip", table = "units") ds <- list_datasets() info <- get_dataset_info("abecip") names(info$categories) #> [1] "sbpe"  "units"  "cgi"

title: Getting Started with realestatebr
text: source argument get_dataset() controls data comes . default ("auto") checks local cache first, falls back GitHub release. Typically, best option use default "github". Choosing "fresh" download data original source: guarantees recent data, slower. Cache files stored user data directory can inspected list_cached_files() cleared clear_user_cache().
code: get_dataset("abecip", source = "cache")    # local cache (instant, works offline) get_dataset("abecip", source = "github")   # GitHub release get_dataset("abecip", source = "fresh")    # direct from the original source

title: Getting Started with realestatebr
text: SBPE (Sistema Brasileiro de Poupança e Empréstimo) primary funding mechanism residential mortgages Brazil. table sbpe fromabecip` tracks deposits withdrawals saving accounts, help finance real estate construction acquisition. plot shows annual net savings flow recent years.  companion table "units" contains monthly counts financed units. plot shows amount units financed per month together LOESS trend line.
code: sbpe <- get_dataset("abecip", table = "sbpe")  glimpse(sbpe) #> Rows: 540 #> Columns: 15 #> $ date              <date> 1982-01-01, 1982-02-01, 1982-03-01, 1982-04-01, 198… #> $ sbpe_inflow       <dbl> 238234.1, 224080.0, 247218.8, 264925.0, 227636.3, 31… #> $ sbpe_outflow      <dbl> 261523.1, 161176.0, 118662.8, 378395.0, 137201.3, 15… #> $ sbpe_netflow      <dbl> -23289, 62904, 128556, -113470, 90435, 164739, -9934… #> $ sbpe_netflow_pct  <dbl> -0.009387130, 0.021881448, 0.043761242, -0.037006429… #> $ sbpe_yield        <dbl> 417103, 0, 0, 485995, 0, 0, 642432, 0, 0, 957944, 0,… #> $ sbpe_stock        <dbl> 2874764, 2937668, 3066224, 3438749, 3529184, 3693923… #> $ rural_inflow      <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ rural_outflow     <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ rural_netflow     <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ rural_netflow_pct <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ rural_yield       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ rural_stock       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ total_stock       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … #> $ total_netflow     <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … # Annual net credit flow sbpe_annual <- sbpe |>   filter(date >= as.Date("2019-01-01")) |>   mutate(year = lubridate::year(date)) |>   summarise(net_flow = sum(sbpe_netflow, na.rm = TRUE) / 1e3, .by = year) |>   mutate(     label_num = format(round(net_flow, 1)),     ypos = if_else(net_flow > 0, net_flow + 10, net_flow - 10)     )  ggplot(sbpe_annual, aes(year, net_flow)) +   geom_col(fill = color_palette[1], alpha = 0.9, width = 0.8) +   geom_text(aes(y = ypos, label = label_num), size = 3) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2019:2026) +   labs(     title = "Annual Net Savings Flow (SBPE)",     x = NULL,     y = "R$ billions"   ) +   theme_series() units <- get_dataset("abecip", table = "units")  glimpse(units) #> Rows: 291 #> Columns: 7 #> $ date                  <date> 2002-01-01, 2002-02-01, 2002-03-01, 2002-04-01,… #> $ units_construction    <dbl> 200, 483, 1049, 684, 571, 1109, 216, 506, 1698, … #> $ units_acquisition     <dbl> 1455, 1456, 1522, 1723, 1536, 1536, 1706, 1838, … #> $ units_total           <dbl> 1655, 1939, 2571, 2407, 2107, 2645, 1922, 2344, … #> $ currency_construction <dbl> 13.540470, 32.117295, 62.592800, 44.422429, 23.4… #> $ currency_acquisition  <dbl> 83.95237, 96.12279, 101.71222, 108.14803, 98.281… #> $ currency_total        <dbl> 97.49284, 128.24008, 164.30502, 152.57046, 121.7… # SBPE units financed per year units_recent <- units |>   filter(date >= as.Date("2019-01-01"))  ggplot(units_recent, aes(date, units_total)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = color_palette[1]) +   geom_smooth(     color = color_palette[1],     lwd = 0.7,     se = FALSE,     method = stats::loess,     method.args = list(span = 0.4)) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "Monthly Financed Units",     y = "Units"   ) +   theme_series()

title: Getting Started with realestatebr
text: bcb_realestate dataset imports real estate statistics Brazilian Central Bank. relatively large dataset exploring can cumbersome. series uniquely identified date series_info. Helper functions v1, v2, …, v5, abbrev_state, category, type provided simplify use dataset. code shows access specific series also fetch group related series. single series shows values SFH (specific credit line).  grouped series show entire household credit stock credit line.  final warning, note bcb_realestate dataset follows YYYY-MM-DD format using last day month default value (e.g. 2023-01-31). can cause issues merging datasets, since first day month common date format (e.g. 2023-01-01). avoid , use lubridate::floor_date(date, 'month'). Future versions realestatebr might provide default behavior.
code: bcb <- get_dataset("bcb_realestate")  # Get a specific series sfh_pf <- bcb |>   filter(series_info == "credito_estoque_carteira_credito_pf_sfh_br")  # Get the all the related series for 'estoque_carteira_credito_pf' credit_stock <- bcb |>   filter(     category == "credito",     type == "estoque",     v1 == "carteira",     v2 == "credito",     v3 == "pf",     # since v4 is left blank, we get all credit lines     v5 == "br"   )  # The helper columns essentially separate the 'series_info' column allowing # for easier filtering. It's equivalent to filtering by regex credit_stock <- bcb |>   filter(grepl(     "(?<=credito_estoque_carteira_credito_pf_).+_br$",     series_info,     perl = TRUE   )) ggplot(sfh_pf, aes(date, value / 1e9)) +   geom_line(lwd = 0.7, color = color_palette[1]) +   labs(title = "SFH", y = "R$ (billions)") +   theme_series() credit_labels <- c(   "Home Equity" = "home-equity",   "Comercial" = "comercial",   "Livre" = "livre",   "FGTS" = "fgts",   "SFH" = "sfh" )  credit_stock <- credit_stock |>   mutate(     credit_line_label = factor(       v4,       levels = credit_labels,       labels = names(credit_labels)     )   )  ggplot(credit_stock, aes(date, value / 1e9)) +   geom_area(aes(fill = credit_line_label), alpha = 0.9) +   scale_fill_manual(values = rev(color_palette[1:5])) +   scale_x_date(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01))) +   scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +   labs(     title = "Real Estate Credit Stock",     subtitle = "Household real estate credit stock (total debt) by credit line",     y = "R$ (billions)",     fill = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: two consistent indices IGMI-R (sale prices) IVAR (rent prices). available option commercial prices FipeZap. indices sourced good datasets built robust methodology. IGMI-R hedonic price index based transaction prices housing mortgages. shortcoming index relatively short time span (2014-present) geographic coverage (major cities ). IVAR repeat-rent index based residential rental contracts. Unfortunately, dataset short time span (2019-present) even limited geographic coverage (4 cities). FipeZap broadest geographic (50+ cities) temporal coverage (2008-present), based median listing prices rather transaction prices. result, index much less reliable. IGMI-R IVAR precise indices sales rent, respectively, used applications.

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: get dataset, use get_dataset() easier comparison indices, table parameter accepts "sale" "rent" argument, stacks available indices single tibble.
code: igmi <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "igmi") ivar <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "ivar") fipezap <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "fipezap") sale <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "sale") rent <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "rent")

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: IVG-R (Índice de Valores de Garantia de Imóveis Residenciais) published Brazilian Central Bank. covers national market 2001 widely regarded official RPPI Brazil. index based median appraisal prices mortgage contracts, smoothed 3-month moving average HP filter.

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: IGMI-R (Índice Geral Mercado Imobiliário Residencial) published Abecip/FGV. uses hedonic pricing model applied actual transaction prices, making methodologically rigorous city-level index available. Coverage spans major Brazilian cities 2014.
code: igmi <- get_dataset("rppi", "igmi")  glimpse(igmi) #> Rows: 1,617 #> Columns: 5 #> $ date      <date> 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> "São Paulo", "Rio De Janeiro", "Belo Horizonte", "Fortaleza"… #> $ index     <dbl> 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.0… #> $ chg       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 0.0003654000, 0.… #> $ acum12m   <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … main_cities <- c("São Paulo", "Rio De Janeiro", "Belo Horizonte", "Brasília")  subigmi <- igmi |>   filter(name_muni %in% main_cities)  ggplot(subigmi, aes(date, index, color = name_muni)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     title = "IGMI-R — Sale Index by City",     x = NULL,     y = "Index",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: FipeZap broadest RPPI terms geographic coverage, indices 50 cities. based median listing prices rather transaction prices. Available 2008 residential commercial markets, breakdowns number bedrooms. Prices stratified region income level, using Census weights, final series smoothed using 3-month moving average. Key columns specific FipeZap: market (residential/commercial), rent_sale (rent/sale), rooms (1, 2, 3, 4+, total), variable (index, chg, acum12m, price_m2, yield). Working dataset requires complex filtering. use cases, however, main choices selecting market (either ‘residential’ ‘commercial’), rent_sale (either ‘sale’ ‘rent’), desired variable.
code: fz <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "fipezap")  glimpse(fz) #> Rows: 677,160 #> Columns: 7 #> $ date      <date> 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", … #> $ market    <chr> "residential", "residential", "residential", "residential", … #> $ rent_sale <chr> "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sal… #> $ variable  <chr> "index", "index", "index", "index", "index", "chg", "chg", "… #> $ rooms     <chr> "total", "1", "2", "3", "4", "total", "1", "2", "3", "4", "t… #> $ value     <dbl> 41.81107, 39.46609, 40.45048, 43.47948, 47.09432, NA, NA, NA… subzap <- fz |>   filter(     market == "residential",     rent_sale == "sale",     rooms == "total",     variable == "index",     name_muni %in% main_cities   ) ggplot(subzap, aes(date, value, color = name_muni)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     title = "FipeZap — Residential Sale Index",     x = NULL,     y = "Index",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: mentioned , table parameter accepts "sale" argument, stacks available indices single tibble. convenience, stacked version dataset implicitly includes rooms == "total" FipeZap. Also, “Brazil” (spelled ‘z’) listed name_muni, shows nation-wide index, instead city specific ones. plot shows three sale indexes national level. ’s convergence indices, growing gap post-pandemic years. highlights importance choosing right index use case.
code: sale_indices <- get_dataset("rppi", "sale")  glimpse(sale_indices) #> Rows: 14,457 #> Columns: 6 #> $ source    <chr> "IGMI-R", "IGMI-R", "IGMI-R", "IGMI-R", "IGMI-R", "IGMI-R", … #> $ date      <date> 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01, 2014-01-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> "São Paulo", "Rio De Janeiro", "Belo Horizonte", "Fortaleza"… #> $ index     <dbl> 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.00000, 100.0… #> $ chg       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 0.0003654000, 0.… #> $ acum12m   <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … comp_index <- sale_indices |>   filter(name_muni == "Brazil", date >= as.Date("2015-01-01")) ggplot(comp_index, aes(date, acum12m * 100, color = source)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   labs(     title = "Comparing Sale Indices in Brazil",     subtitle = "12-month accumulated change (%)",     y = "%",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: IVAR (Índice de Variação de Aluguéis Residenciais) published FGV uses repeat-rent methodology applied actual rental contracts. covers major Brazilian cities 2019 available city national level. Notably, index smoothing, making rather volatile. plot, add 5-month moving average using trendseries package.
code: ivar <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "ivar")  glimpse(ivar) #> Rows: 440 #> Columns: 5 #> $ date      <date> 2018-12-01, 2018-12-01, 2018-12-01, 2018-12-01, 2018-12-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> NA, "São Paulo", "Rio De Janeiro", "Belo Horizonte", "Porto … #> $ index     <dbl> 100.000, 100.000, 100.000, 100.000, 100.000, 99.852, 99.731,… #> $ chg       <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, -1.480000e-03, -2.690000e-03, 8.310000e-… #> $ acum12m   <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, … library(trendseries)  ivar_trend <- ivar |>   filter(name_muni != "Brazil") |>   augment_trends(     value_col = "index",     group_cols = "name_muni",     method = "ma",     window = 5     ) ggplot(ivar_trend, aes(date, color = name_muni)) +   geom_line(aes(y = index), lwd = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(aes(y = trend_ma), lwd = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "IVAR — City Rent Indices",     subtitle = "Smoothed moving average (5-month window)",     x = NULL,     y = "Index"   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: QuintoAndar published two successive rental indices São Paulo Rio de Janeiro. IQA (2019–mid-2023) based median contract prices. mid-2023 rebranded IQAIW (Índice QuintoAndar ImovelWeb) adopted hedonic model incorporating listing contract prices. two series directly comparable due methodology break. original IQA compared median contract prices, stratified region income level (using Census weights, similar FipeZap). IQAIW uses hedonic model (spatial GAM), double imputation, similar Census-based stratification. Notably, IQA returns rent prices (R$/m²) rather index, base-100 normalisation needed build index. convenience, distribution includes simple index based first available value.  newer IQAIW index cities , similar FipeZap, bedroom stratification (rooms).  Finally, note IQAIW effectively newer (improved) version older IQA index, kept historical purposes.
code: iqa <- get_dataset("rppi", "iqa") iqaiw <- get_dataset("rppi", "iqaiw")  glimpse(iqaiw) #> Rows: 1,660 #> Columns: 6 #> $ date      <date> 2019-05-01, 2019-10-01, 2019-11-01, 2019-12-01, 2020-01-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> "Belo Horizonte", "Belo Horizonte", "Belo Horizonte", "Belo … #> $ rooms     <chr> "total", "total", "total", "total", "total", "total", "total… #> $ index     <dbl> 100.0000, 100.1882, 100.4708, 100.6167, 101.3517, 102.6531, … #> $ chg       <dbl> NA, 0.003514777, 0.002820158, 0.001452626, 0.007304582, 0.01… #> $ acum12m   <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 0.07024414, 0.07004492, 0.07… ggplot(iqa, aes(date, index, color = name_muni)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "IQA — Rent Index",     subtitle = "Index (2019/06 = 100)",     y = "Index",     color = NULL   ) +     theme_series() ggplot(subset(iqaiw, rooms == "total" & !is.na(acum12m)), aes(date, acum12m * 100, color = name_muni)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "IQAIW — Rent Index",     subtitle = "Accumulated 12-month change (%)",     y = "%",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series() quintoandar <- bind_rows(   list("IQA" = iqa, "IQAIW" = iqaiw),   .id = "source" )  quintoandar_spo <- quintoandar |>   filter(name_muni == "São Paulo", !rooms %in% c("1", "2", "3")) ggplot(quintoandar_spo, aes(date, index, color = source)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "QuintoAndar Rent Index — São Paulo",     subtitle = "IQA (pre-2023) and IQAIW (post-2023) use different methodologies",     x = NULL,     y = "Index",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: FipeZap provides broadest geographic coverage rental indices, based median listing prices rather contracts. example shows 12-month accumulated change across cities complete data 2021. Note table one used previous sales example. plot emphasizes main strength FipeZap’s index: broad geographic coverage.
code: fz <- get_dataset("rppi", table = "fipezap")  glimpse(fz) #> Rows: 677,160 #> Columns: 7 #> $ date      <date> 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01, 2008-01-01,… #> $ name_muni <chr> "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", "Brazil", … #> $ market    <chr> "residential", "residential", "residential", "residential", … #> $ rent_sale <chr> "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sale", "sal… #> $ variable  <chr> "index", "index", "index", "index", "index", "chg", "chg", "… #> $ rooms     <chr> "total", "1", "2", "3", "4", "total", "1", "2", "3", "4", "t… #> $ value     <dbl> 41.81107, 39.46609, 40.45048, 43.47948, 47.09432, NA, NA, NA… fz_rent <- fz |>   filter(     market == "residential",     rent_sale == "rent",     rooms == "total",     variable == "acum12m",     date >= as.Date("2019-01-01")   )  sel_cities <- fz_rent |>   filter(date == "2019-01-01", !is.na(value)) |>   pull(name_muni) ggplot(subset(fz_rent, name_muni %in% sel_cities), aes(date, value * 100)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, color = color_palette[1]) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +   facet_wrap(vars(name_muni)) +   labs(     title = "FipeZap — 12-month Rent Change by City",     x = NULL,     y = "Accumulated 12-month change (%)"   ) +   theme_series()

code: rent_indices <- get_dataset("rppi", "rent") rent_indices_comp <- rent_indices |>   filter(     name_muni %in% c("São Paulo", "Rio de Janeiro"),     date >= as.Date("2019-01-01")   ) ggplot(rent_indices_comp, aes(date, acum12m, color = source)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +   facet_wrap(vars(name_muni)) +   scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +   labs(     title = "Rent Indices — São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro",     subtitle = "12-month accumulated change",     y = "Accumulated change",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: index base period, normalisation required comparing levels. example rebases national sale indices January 2018 = 100.
code: sales <- get_dataset("rppi", "sale")  national <- sales |>   filter(name_muni == "Brazil", date >= as.Date("2018-01-01"))  national_rebased <- national |>   mutate(     index_rebased = index / first(index) * 100,     .by = source   )  total_growth <- national_rebased |>   summarise(     growth = last(index_rebased) - first(index_rebased),     date = last(date),     index_rebased = last(index_rebased),     .by = source   ) |>   mutate(label = sprintf("%s:\n+%.1f%%", source, growth)) ggplot(national_rebased, aes(date, index_rebased, color = source)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +   geom_label(     data = total_growth,     aes(label = label),     hjust = 0,     nudge_x = 30,     nudge_y = c(-5, 10, -5),     show.legend = FALSE,     size = 3   ) +   scale_x_date(     date_breaks = "1 year",     date_labels = "%Y",     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.125))   ) +   labs(     title = "Brazil National Sale Indices — Rebased to Jan 2018",     x = NULL,     y = "Index (Jan 2018 = 100)",     color = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Working with Property Price Indices
text: post-pandemic divergence rental sale markets one discussed trends Brazilian real estate. FipeZap, covers markets, makes comparison straightforward.
code: fz <- get_dataset("rppi", "fipezap")  cities <- c("São Paulo", "Rio De Janeiro", "Belo Horizonte") base_year <- 2019  base_avg <- fz |>   filter(     name_muni %in% cities,     market   == "residential",     rooms    == "total",     variable == "index",     lubridate::year(date) == base_year   ) |>   summarise(base = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .by = c(name_muni, rent_sale))  fz_divergence <- fz |>   filter(     name_muni %in% cities,     market   == "residential",     rooms    == "total",     variable == "index",     date     >= as.Date("2019-01-01")   ) |>   left_join(base_avg, by = c("name_muni", "rent_sale")) |>   mutate(idx = 100 * value / base) ggplot(fz_divergence, aes(date, idx, color = rent_sale)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.4) +   facet_wrap(vars(name_muni)) +   labs(     title    = "Post-pandemic Rent vs Sale Divergence",     subtitle = "FipeZap residential index, 2019 average = 100",     x        = NULL,     y        = "Index",     color    = NULL   ) +   theme_series()

title: Import Brazilian Real Estate Data into R

title: Import Brazilian Real Estate Data into R
text: two key functions realestatebr: get_dataset() list_datasets(). former retrieves data, latter lists available datasets. get_dataset() expects name (optional) table argument. table specified, function returns first available table.
code: library(realestatebr)  # Discover available datasets datasets <- list_datasets()  # Get specific table sbpe <- get_dataset(name = "abecip", table = "sbpe")  # Get property price indices fipezap <- get_dataset("rppi", "fipezap")

(%)",     color = ""   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     palette.colour.discrete = c("#1E3A5F", "#4A90C2", "#2C7A7B")   )

IQAIW developed 2023 replaced former IQA index. Given change methodology data sources, IQAIW directly comparable IQA index. Formally, index hedonic double imputed index, controlling quality changes using flexible GAM specification location variables. sense, IQAIW theoretically sound median stratified indices like FipeZap former IQA. mixture listings contracts, however, lacks theoretical support seems mainly driven branding purposes. ImovelWeb brand purchased QuintoAndar 2021-22 IQAIW symbolizes merging brands. words, original IQA 've improved simply adopting hedonic methodology, without need mix data sources.

text: Fixed SECOVI dataset return categories default instead “condo” Problem: get_dataset("secovi") returning “condo” category (1,939 rows) instead categories (9,398 rows). caused test failures launch/rent/sale tables. Root Cause: table parameter specified, code defaulted first category alphabetically (“condo”), rather fetching categories.
